results.title: Christmas
results.brief_description: Adapted from Chapter 7 of the book “More about Paddington” by Michael Bond. For Paddington’s first Christmas with the Brown’s, he receives a chemistry set, a scrapbook and a xylophone. By 6 o’clock Christmas morning he is proudly banging out the National Anthem for everyone to hear. Paddington has never eaten a meal like Mrs Brown’s Christmas dinner, but what an eventful dinner it turns out to be. Animated by Ivor Wood and told by Michael Hordern. Suitable for lower and middle primary levels.

results.title: Christmas
results.brief_description: A special program for Christmas featuring children from Ivanhoe East Primary School. The program covers the making of Christmas decorations for the tree, making the traditional Christmas sweet, “White Christmas”, followed by a Christmas story and Christmas carols.

results.title: Choose with care
results.brief_description: This is an information and training program designed to assist organisations establish a safe environment for the children and young people in their care such as child care centres, youth groups, sports clubs, before and after school care programs and all other organisations working with children and young people. It provides a proactive, preventative and participatory approach to building and maintaining child safe organisations. A comprehensive handbook is also included in this package.

results.title: Chinese take away [Widescreen]
results.creator_credit: Mitzi Goldman, Looking Glass Pictures and SBS Independent
results.brief_description: Written and performed by Anna Yen, and adapted for television from the original stage play, “Chinese take away” tells the story about Anna’s grandmother, her mother and herself through a unique and unusual blend of poetic storytelling, re-enactments, stills and archival footage, and artistically evocative images of movement and surrealism. Through her interpretive stories, Anna plays out the characters of her grandmother, mother and her father to seek out the truth in the hope of understanding her family on a more intimate and personal level - to put herself in their shoes. This journey of discovery spans several decades from early 1900s China and 1930s Hong Kong, through to 1960s suburban Sydney and into the present day. But it is the story of her mother that is particularly moving and Anna’s captivating portrayal as she reveals her mother’s inner thoughts and emotions and the tragic circumstances that became her life. This is compelling viewing, with powerful and mesmerising visuals, a haunting soundtrack, and human stories of personal history and cultural heritage, themes often used by award-winning director, Mitzi Goldman (Hatred, Snakes and ladders, Things I call mine). Cast also includes Natasha Chiew (young Anna), Natalie Tjen (teenage Anna), Eve Wang (young mother), Maggie Chung (young grandmother). Cinematography by Himman Dhamija.

results.title: China: times of silk and gold (TEFC)
results.creator_credit: Michael Gill and British Broadcasting Corporation
results.brief_description: Professor William Watson, Professor of Chinese Art and Archaeology at the University of London, and Magnus Magnusson continue their journey across modern China, visiting one of the world’s great Buddhist shrines; the caves at Lyang; Sian, the ancient capital; and the tomb of a T’ang princess who died in 701 AD In the Forbidden City of Kubblah Khan in Peking, a remarkable clock is discovered - a present to the Emperor from Britain’s first trade mission in the 18th century.

results.title: China: times of jade and bronze (TEFC)
results.creator_credit: Michael Gill and British Broadcasting Corporation
results.brief_description: In 1973 an exhibition of Chinese art was presented at the Royal Academy in London. Many of the prize pieces appear in this film, which evolves into an extensive journey through modern China to visit many of the sites from which the treasures came, putting them into the context of place and history. With William Watson and Magnus Magnusson. Museum sequences directed by Ray Sutcliffe, photographed by Phil Meheux.
